question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: poll(PollingContext context)] Can an unprivileged external user set endTime in the TopicMessageFilter to a value that is only 1 nanosecond after startTime, causing each poll to return at most one message, and by opening many such narrow-window subscriptions in parallel, reconstruct the topic history in a different order than the original consensus sequence by interleaving results from concurrent polls?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the METRIC constant 'hiero_mirror_grpc_retriever' is used for all retrieve() calls regardless of topicId, causing metric aggregation to obscure per-topic retrieval anomalies and allowing an attacker to hide history-reorganization attempts behind normal-looking aggregate metrics?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: poll(PollingContext context)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Flux.fromStream() wrapping of topicMessageRepository.findByFilter() — which materializes the entire result set into memory before streaming — to cause a memory pressure scenario where the JVM GC pauses mid-stream delivery, causing messages to be delivered in bursts that appear reordered relative to their consensusTimestamp sequence?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the withFixedDelay(context.getFrequency()) polling interval — 2 seconds for throttled, 20ms for unthrottled — to time their subscription such that messages inserted between two poll cycles appear in the next poll's result set but after messages from the current poll, creating an apparent reordering relative to insertion order?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: onNext(TopicMessage topicMessage)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that onNext() updates last (the cursor) based on the order messages are delivered by the reactive stream — not by consensusTimestamp — to cause the cursor to advance to a message that arrived out of DB-insertion order, causing subsequent polls to skip messages with lower consensusTimestamp values that were inserted late?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the doOnCancel(context::onComplete) handler to trigger the onComplete logging path without actually completing the stream, and by rapidly canceling and resubscribing, cause the subscriberId-based log entries to show a misleading history of message counts that obscures actual history reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: poll(PollingContext context)] Can an unprivileged external user set the TopicMessageFilter.limit to exactly 1 and exploit the special-case hint suppression in findByFilter (limit != 1 skips the random_page_cost hint), causing the DB query planner to choose a different index scan order for limit=1 vs limit>1, resulting in different message orderings being returned for otherwise identical queries?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Schedulers.boundedElastic() scheduler shared across all PollingTopicMessageRetriever instances to cause scheduler starvation, where some subscriptions' poll() calls are delayed while others proceed normally, resulting in different subscribers receiving the same topic's history in different orders?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: isComplete()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the limitHit check (filter.getLimit() == total.get()) using exact equality — rather than >= — to cause an off-by-one scenario where total overshoots limit due to a race condition, making isComplete() never return true for the limit path and causing the stream to deliver more messages than requested, including messages beyond the intended history window?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: poll(PollingContext context)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the newFilter is built with filter.toBuilder() which copies all original filter fields including topicId, and by providing a topicId that maps to a topic with a very large message history, cause the pageSize calculation to always return maxPageSize, keeping the retriever in an infinite polling loop that continuously reorganizes the apparent history window?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the interaction between retryWhen(Retry.backoff(Long.MAX_VALUE, ...)) and timeout() to create a scenario where a retry attempt starts just before the timeout fires, causing the stream to emit a partial second batch of messages after the timeout signal, resulting in messages being delivered in two separate bursts that appear to reorganize the history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: poll(PollingContext context)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the context.getPageSize().set(0L) reset at the start of each poll() call — which is not atomic with the subsequent DB query — to cause isComplete() to read pageSize=0 during the reset window and prematurely terminate the stream, dropping all messages that would have been returned by the current poll?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TopicMessageFilter.subscriberId is randomly generated per filter instance (RandomStringUtils.random(8,...)) and is used only for logging, to create many subscriptions with different subscriberIds that all query the same topic history, making it impossible to correlate log entries and detect that a single attacker is probing the history from multiple angles?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: poll(PollingContext context)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ordering guarantee of findByFilter (ORDER BY consensusTimestamp ASC) combined with a startTime set to a future timestamp that then becomes past during the subscription lifetime, causing the first poll to return no messages and subsequent polls to return messages in a different apparent order than subscribers who started earlier?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the TopicMessageFilter.isValidStartTime() validation (startTime <= DomainUtils.now()) by submitting a startTime that is valid at filter creation time but becomes stale by the time the first poll executes, causing the retriever to include messages that were not yet committed when the subscription was initiated, reorganizing the apparent history boundary?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: poll(PollingContext context)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that poll() uses Flux.fromStream() which eagerly materializes the DB result set, and by subscribing to a topic with millions of messages, cause the stream to hold a stale snapshot of the DB in memory while new messages are committed, then deliver the stale snapshot followed by live messages in a way that appears to reorganize the consensus sequence?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the interaction between the throttled path (numRepeats=Long.MAX_VALUE) and the unthrottled path (numRepeats=maxPolls=12) by manipulating which path is selected — if the throttled flag is determined by caller logic — to receive a different number of historical messages than expected, creating an incomplete or extended history view?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: onNext(TopicMessage topicMessage)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequenceNumber-based deduplication filter in TopicMessageServiceImpl (t.getSequenceNumber() > last.getSequenceNumber()) combined with the consensusTimestamp-based cursor in poll(), to create a scenario where a message with a higher consensusTimestamp but lower sequenceNumber is silently dropped, creating a permanent gap in the delivered history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: poll(PollingContext context)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the limit in poll() is cast to int from a long subtraction (filter.getLimit() - context.getTotal().get()), and by setting limit to a value between Integer.MAX_VALUE+1 and Long.MAX_VALUE, cause the cast to produce a negative int that is then passed to Math.min(), resulting in a negative pageSize that causes the DB query to return all messages without a limit, reorganizing the history delivery?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Micrometer observation tap (Micrometer.observation(observationRegistry)) to infer timing information about when specific historical messages are retrieved, and use this side-channel to determine the consensusTimestamp distribution of a topic's history without being authorized to read the message content?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: poll(PollingContext context)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the DB query hint (set local random_page_cost = 0) is applied as a native query executeUpdate() before the main query, and by timing their subscription to coincide with a concurrent transaction that holds a lock on the topic_message table, cause the hint to be applied in a different transaction context, leading to a different query plan and different message ordering?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that retrieve() is a cold publisher that creates a new PollingContext per subscription, and by opening subscriptions at different times for the same topic, receive different historical message sets due to DB state changes between subscriptions, making the topic's history appear non-deterministic and reorganized?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: isComplete()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the throttled isComplete() condition (pageSize.get() < retrieverProperties.getMaxPageSize()) where maxPageSize defaults to 1000, by subscribing to a topic that consistently has exactly 1000 messages per poll window, causing isComplete() to always return false and the retriever to poll indefinitely, consuming server resources while delivering an ever-growing history that includes future messages mixed with historical ones?
